title: Rent vs Buy Calculator
meta_title: Rent vs Buy Calculator — Find the Exact Break-Even Year | CalcForgeTools
meta_description: Discover mathematically when buying a home beats renting. Interactive crossover chart shows the exact year buying wins. Includes appreciation, investment returns, and hidden costs.

hero.title: Rent vs Buy Calculator
hero.subtitle: The answer depends on how long you plan to stay. See the exact year where buying begins to beat renting on the interactive crossover chart.

inputs.name: investmentReturn
inputs.label: Investment Return if Renting (% per year net)

inputs.name: maintenancePct
inputs.label: Annual Ownership Costs (% of value — taxes, insurance, repairs)

inputs.name: horizonYears
inputs.label: Analysis Horizon (years you plan to live there)

formula: Buy Cost = Down + Payments + Maintenance − Appreciation | Rent Cost = Σ Rent − Investment Returns
formula_explanation: This calculator compares two scenarios over time: (1) BUY — you pay a down payment, monthly mortgage installments with interest, and maintenance (taxes, insurance, repairs), but build equity through property appreciation. (2) RENT — you pay increasing rent each month, but invest the difference (down payment + monthly savings) in a portfolio earning compound returns. The chart shows when the buyer's total wealth surpasses the renter's.
example: A $350,000 home with $70,000 down at 6.5% over 30 years creates payments of ~$1,770/mo. If rent is $1,800/mo with 3% increases, and you invest the difference at 7%, buying beats renting around year 7.

faq.question: What is the general rule for renting vs buying?
faq.answer: If you stay longer than 5-8 years, buying tends to win. Under 5 years, renting and investing the difference is usually better. But every scenario is different — use this calculator to find your personal break-even.

faq.question: What is the 'crossover year' on the graph?
faq.answer: It is the year when the buyer's net equity (home value minus remaining mortgage) surpasses the renter's accumulated investment portfolio. Before this point, renting was mathematically better.

faq.question: Why include maintenance costs?
faq.answer: Homeowners pay property taxes, insurance, HOA fees, and repairs that renters don't. These typically add 1-2% of the property value per year and significantly affect the long-term comparison.

faq.question: What if I buy with cash?
faq.answer: Set the Down Payment equal to the Property Value and the Term to 0. The calculator will account for the opportunity cost of that invested capital.
